chunks.thread_subject: Does my 1 Victory Point count in the same turn when it was bought?
chunks.embed_text: If a player buys a Development card that contains a 1 Victory Point, does that Victory Point count immediately, or does it have to wait until the next turn? Can a player reveal a Victory Point card immediately after purchasing it to win the game?
chunks.content: According to the rules, Victory Point cards are exempt from the restriction that prevents players from using Development cards in the same turn they were bought. Players can reveal Victory Point cards immediately after purchasing them, and if this brings their total points to 10 or more, they can win the game. The rules state that players may reveal Victory Point cards during their turn and when they are sure they have 10 victory points, which is to win the game.

raw_thread.body:
Hi guys,

Thanks a mill in advance for helping us out here. The following led to a heated discussion at our Catan game last night and we're out to find the answer!  

It's my turn. Whilst on 9 points, I bought a Development card which contained the 1 Victory Point - and thus my winning 10th point. Does that Victory Point gets 'activated' straight away e.g. I won immediately due to that card, or would I have to wait till my next turn before it counts? 

Thnks so much!

Thanks a mill in advance for helping us out here. The following led to a heated discussion at our Catan game last night and we're out to find the answer!

Thnks so much!

Normally you're not allowed to use a development card in the same turn that it was bought. However, the VP cards are exempt from that restriction.
So, you're allowed to play them immediately or wait to reveal them until the end of the game.
Cool!!! That's what I thought, but my opponents wouldn't believe me (of course ha, now they have to accept they lost). Thanks!

Did you check the rules book? It's supposed to be mentioned in there as well. The thing with Catan is that because it's been around for 25 years the rules have differed from edition to edition, so it's also dependant on which edition you have, but I'm pretty sure this particular rule has been the same.

Normally you're not allowed to use a development card in the same turn that it was bought. However, the VP cards are exempt from that restriction.

In versions that use the standard set of development cards, victory points aren't revealed except at the end of the game. There is no rule Apparently in newer editions, there is a rule to stop you from revealing them earlier, and demonstrating the lack of a monopoly card might occasionally make trading a little bit easier for you, but that's about the only benefit that revealing your VP cards may have.

Announcing your victory does not count as a use of development cards. In theory, you could buy several cards at once, find more than one VP and thereby finish the game. Possibly after playing another development card that gave you Longest Road, Largest Army, or the resources to buy that many development cards.

I cannot say for sure that this is wrong (for instant I don't own the game so I cannot check the rules). But I have played Catan a lot online (using the Windows games application) and in that game you cannot get your 1 extra point until one turn after you have purchased your Development Card.

Not that I think it makes much difference playing either way. In some very unique situations it might make a difference though.
I would suggest that this thing is discussed before the game starts so that everyone plays with the same rules.

Luk Van You technically never play victory point cards. You can just show them to claim the win. But a knight is not a victory point card. You need to actually play your third knight before you can claim the largest army.

Was there are reason VP cards are just "shown" vs "played"?

The reason victory point cards are "revealed" and not "played" is that you are only allowed to play 1 development card on your turn.  (This only applies to development cards which do things other than directly provide victory points.)

You can reveal all of your victory point development cards on your turn (in order to bring your total victory points up to 10+).  This can be in addition to the 1 development card you play.

So, the distinction exists to help remind players that you do not have to use your sole development card "play" for the victory point cards you have.

chunks.thread_subject: Longest Road vs. Largest Army
chunks.embed_text: How do the Longest Road and Largest Army cards work in the game of Catan, specifically in situations where a player's road is tied or broken by another player's settlement?
chunks.content: The Longest Road card is removed from the game if another player builds a road of equal length, until a new longest road emerges. However, if a player's longest road is broken by a settlement, the card is only removed if the player is no longer tied for the longest road. The Largest Army card, on the other hand, remains with the original owner in the event of a tie, and only gets transferred if another player builds a larger army.

chunks.thread_subject: Clarification of Harbor rules
chunks.embed_text: Can a harbor in the game of Catan be accessed from two or three settlements along its hex? How do the harbor rules work in relation to the hex edges and intersections?
chunks.content: A harbor can only be accessed from two settlements along its hex, specifically the two intersections on the same edge of the hex where the harbor is located. This is because harbors are features of an edge, not the whole tile. Only one player can claim each harbor.

raw_thread.body:
I came here searching for a clarification of a rule, and surprisingly saw no post directly about it. (There were a few that indirectly addressed the question, but it took me some time to find them) Since I came up with this question and others might wonder about the same thing I thought I might go ahead and make a post.

The answer may seem obvious, but I was wondering if a harbor can only be accessed from two settlements along its hex, or three. On the frames used by the most recent editions, there are jettys adjacent to two of the intersections of the hex, but there is also an intersection on the same coast that looks like it could be used, according to the stated rules, but there is no jetty.

I always assumed that only the jettys could be used, which is correct, but I was was reading a review of 3D Catan that caused me to question that assumption. I came here to ask that question, or at least get clarification that I was right.

My solution was to realize that harbors are on the EDGE of the hex on which they are located, not the whole tile. This can be clearly seen in previous editions of Catan, where the sea hexes along the border clearly marked the two intersections where a settlement may be placed. The change from hexes to frame pieces obscured that factor, but harbors are still along a single edge of a the frame.

To put the nail in the coffin, the harbor pieces in Seafarers are clearly placed adjacent to an edge of a shoreline hex. There is no need for confusion as long as you understand the harbor is a feature of an edge.

This if course means that only one player can claim each harbor, which I'm sure was intended. 

(To clarify why I was confused, the harbors in 3D Catan are boats, with 3D cargo loaded into them, which I really like. But each boat had a pair of gangplanks, and the reviewer just placed them haphazardly, making it look like they were leading to two adjacent hexes. This was wrong; the boats were supposed to be placed with the gangplanks leading to the two vertices of the same edge)
You are correct that the harbors could only have at most one settlement each. They are meant to be aligned to an edge as a trade-off for only getting access to one or two production hexes.

chunks.thread_subject: 2 for 1 ports

chunks.content: The community consensus is that 2:1 ports are one-way only, allowing players to trade two of the specified resource for one of any other resource, but not the reverse. This is supported by the majority of posts and is consistent with the rulebook.

Well, you could have a variation that if you upgrade a port settlement to a city, then you can have it both ways. :)
do you have to put a settlement on a port for it to be used? And if the ports a wood port does your road have to include a wood hex?

Yes

I've never played with the "both ways" rule, but it makes thematic sense to me. If I can take my bricks down to the port and trade them for other resources, some merchant at the port must be on the other side of that trade, trading those resources for brick. It makes logical sense to go trade for bricks, too, and our hypothetical merchant would love the opportunity to double up on both sides of the trade.

chunks.thread_subject: Can a settlement intersect another player’s road that only has a settlement on one side?
chunks.embed_text: Can a player build a settlement that intersects another player's road, effectively isolating it, if the road only has a settlement on one side?
chunks.content: The community consensus is that the settlement can be built, and it will separate the red road line for purposes of calculating the Longest Road. However, the isolated road is not 'dead' and the player can still build off it by adding another road first. The official FAQ and rules references support this interpretation.

raw_thread.body:

If someone can point me to a section in the rules, I’d appreciate it, this is a huge sticking point in our game
The rules just say it has to be two away from any other settlement. That point is. So it's allowed.
It's allowed and one way to cut off others routes

So blue CAN build there and isolate that one red road, so it is no longer connected to anything?

Just need some clarification, this is a pretty big sticking point in the game
Yes, blue can build there.

Just need some clarification, this is a pretty big sticking point in the game

See page 9

https://www.catan.com/sites/default/files/2021-06/catan_base...

The placing of a Blue settlement is legal as noted above, and furthermore, it affects Red when considering the Longest Road.  

In your example, if Red constructed a road between the "5" and "4" hexes, thus connecting the 2 existing Red roads coming from the two Red settlements (5 total), Red would be awarded the Longest Road card (unless someone else already held it).  The placing of the Blue settlement in the purple circle would then divide that Red Longest Road into two shorter roads (3 + 2), and Red would lose the Longest Road card.

Just need some clarification, this is a pretty big sticking point in the game

As others have said, Blue can build there, but I wanted to add that while that would leave the red road no longer connected to anything, Red could still build off that road. They'd have to add another road first before a settlement and on the current board that would only get them a 5 wood, but the road isn't "dead" just because it's on its own. 

(Also, it's funny to me that it's Blue that is shown as doing this in your picture. At my house, the person who always plays with that color has earned the name The Evil Blue People because he'll regularly do this, usually 2 roads and a settlement all in one turn. And often on the spot that his mother-in-law has been trying to collect resources to build on for ages! )

Since the OP asked for a rules reference: in the rules linked by Liam above, the picture under “Longest Road” shows exactly this happening, and the text explains that building there will break the road up, as Bill said.
See also the official FAQ at https://www.catan.com/node/1532

Blue can build a settlement there, which separates the red road line for purposes of calculating the Longest Road. Red can however continue to add roads to the isolated road, build settlements there, and might ultimately reconnect that road section to the other section by going around the coastal wood tile with the 5,  and /or connect one the roads to the other red settlement around the brick tile.

In this specific situation, if red wants to continue its longest road strategy it either needs to run around the coast line or connect the two settlements, losing the advantage of two roads already built. It is a setback, but hardly a crushing blow.

chunks.thread_subject: Do you have to “claim” longest road
chunks.embed_text: Do you have to claim the longest road in the game, or is it automatically awarded when a player meets the requirements?
chunks.content: The community resolved that the longest road is automatically awarded when a player meets the requirements, and it is not necessary to claim it. According to the official rules, the first player to build a continuous road of at least 5 road segments receives the special card, and if another player builds a longer road, they immediately take the special card.

raw_thread.body: Do you have to claim longest road or is it issued regardless once you get required road pieces down.
Issued regardless.

Official answer: https://boardgamegeek.com/thread/494190/article/4629712#4629...

You have to do everything the rules state, you cannot "forget" or choose not to claim a resource, longest road, victory etc.

"Claim" (as a choice) may be a misdirection.

According to rules copyright 2020, as found here: "Game Rules for Download" page at Catan.com, it would simply be awarded.

Page 4
The first player to build a continuous road (not counting forks) of at least 5 road segments receives the special card "Longest Road".  If another player succeeds in bulding a longer road than the one created by the current owner of the "Longest Road" card, they immediately take the special card (and its 2 victory points).

There are instances where it has an impact:
1) When a second person reaches 5 they would otherwise be able to claim, even though not entitled to it.
2) Cities and Knights progress cards for example consider people with more VP than you.  Therefore "Not claiming 2 vp" would have a big impact.

To earn those cards you have to overpass the previous maximum count, not just tie them. Also, not claiming any will be always bad, a mistake for the player, because they grant points, nothing bad to chose to hide you awarded them.

chunks.thread_subject: Rules, 1 victory point.
chunks.embed_text: Can a 1 victory point development card be used in the same turn it is purchased in the game of Catan?
chunks.content: The community is divided on the answer, with some citing the rulebook as saying development cards cannot be played the same turn they are purchased, while others point to specific exceptions for victory point cards that allow them to be revealed immediately if they bring the player to 10 points. Some argue that victory point cards are not 'played' but rather owned, and can be revealed at any time.

raw_thread.body:
Hi!
I have a doubt about the rules. Can the 1 victory point development card be used in the same turn it is purchased?

The way to think about it is this: you don't need to use these cards at all. They just count as hidden victory points from the moment you get them. This means there's no limit on the number you can reveal at the same time, or on when you can do so.

So yes, you can immediately 'use' them.

According to page 5 of the rulebook (English version), you cannot play a development card the same turn it is purchased. Therefore if you purchase the game winning victory point you’d need to wait until your next turn to claim victory. The rules also state that you should keep your development cards secret to hide any victory points.
As you indicate, there seems to be a contradiction: is there an official email where I can clear up my doubts?

The Settlers of Catan. Fourth Edition. Game Rules & Almanac Page 7.
"Exception: If you buy a card and it is a Victory Point Card that brings you to 10 points, you may immediately reveal this card (and all other VP cards) and win the game."

In the rules linked on catan.com, page 5, right column under Victory Point Cards, it says at the end:
You may play any number of victory point cards during your turn, even during the turn you purchased them.

Clearly, this is meant to overwrite the general limitations on playing progress cards.
You do not play the VP cards. You simply own them, so the "when you can play..." doesn't apply. If you are at 9VP, and you buy a card, and it's a VP card...you win! Stand up and cheer!

According to page 5 of the rulebook (English version), you cannot play a development card the same turn it is purchased. Therefore if you purchase the game winning victory point you’d need to wait until your next turn to claim victory. The rules also state that you should keep your development cards secret to hide any victory points. 

You do not play the VP cards. You simply own them, so the "when you can play..." doesn't apply. If you are at 9VP, and you buy a card, and it's a VP card...you win! Stand up and cheer!

This also means that if you have 8 points, buy 2 dev cards, and they're both VPs, you still win instantly. The limit on playing more than one dev card per turn doesn't apply to these, because you're not playing them.
Thank you all.

chunks.thread_subject: Building on coast Catan
chunks.embed_text: Can you build a settlement on the coast in Catan, and if so, are there any specific rules or restrictions that apply? The game rules seem to contradict themselves when it comes to building on the coast. How do you determine if you can build a settlement on the coast?
chunks.content: The community resolved that the restriction regarding settlement building is that all settlements must be at least two hex-sides apart, and this rule applies to building on the coast as well. One user explained that the intersections on the coast are treated as if they are adjacent to a free intersection, due to the original design of the game board. Another user provided context by mentioning that older versions of the game had full-size sea hexes instead of a frame.

raw_thread.body: In the Catan rules it says you may only build settlements on an intersection when the 3 adjacent intersections are unoccupied. The game rules fail to mention anything about unoccupied intersections when building on the coast. The coast does not have 3 intersections, only 2 so it is impossible to have 3 unoccupied intersections on the coast. So the game rules contradict themselves because we have no way of knowing how many unoccupied intersections there must be in order to build a settlement on the coast
You can find some good answers to this in this previous thread where you posted the same question.

I'm not sure what rule you're confused about (can you quote it?), but the only restriction regarding settlement building is all settlements must be at least two hex-sides apart. Under those circumstances you can build inland, inland adjacent to the desert, on the coast, or on the coast adjacent to the desert. Under those circumstances, you'll be adjacent to 3, 2, 2, and 1 resource generating areas respectively.

The hexagonal frame for the current edition of Catan was originally a ring of separate water hexes, and the areas on the frame are still treated as such. There is an invisible border to the edge of the frame from which originally two edges would extend outward. In this regard, the intersections pointing outward are adjacent to a free intersection. 

As a consequence, the corners of the frame are regular intersections between two (incompletely represented) water hexes and the land hex, and with a free intersection on the far end of the invisible border between the two water hexes. The "contradiction" is mainly ignorance of the meaning of the frame that may have become slightly less obvious in re-design.
Older versions of SoC had full size sea hexes instead of a frame.

chunks.thread_subject: Is there an "official" variant for an extra round of turns when someone declares victory?
chunks.embed_text: Is there an official variant for an extra round of turns when someone declares victory in the game of CATAN?
chunks.content: The official rule is that once a player reaches 10 victory points during their turn, they win and the game ends immediately. There is no rule to let the rest of the players who did not take their turn this round to do it after the winning player declares their victory. However, players can choose to house rule it if they want to.

raw_thread.body:
I was reviewing the questions & answers on the awesome CATAN FAQ, here:
https://www.catan.com/faq/basegame

I noticed one that really confused me.  Here it is, in full. (I'm including the text and a screenshot.)  See my question following after:
CATAN ⚫ Yes – if the game ends at a point agreed upon by the players before or during the game, after which each player – beginning with the starting player – may take one more turn, that is, the game ends when player 5 or 6 has taken his turn.
In this case, it is recommended to include another special building phase after player 5 or 6 has finished his turn, so that the players can use the resources they just have received. Otherwise, the last player to take a turn is at a disadvantage, because no one will trade with him any more. The order in which the players take their turns would have too much bearing on the outcome of the game, because during the last round the number of trades performed would diminish player by player – after all, why should a player who is no longer allowed to build keep on trading?
This rule is also recommended for 3-4 player games, because otherwise the last player would hardly have an opportunity to trade. Therefore, in a 3-4 player game, a one-time final build phase for all other players would take place.

For the "Yes" option here, what game-end point "agreed upon by the players" do they mean?  Is this a variant I just don't see in the current version of the rules?

It seems like "No" should be the only answer to this question.  Unless it's a house rule or variant.  Or unless I'm missing something else?

The quotes above relate to the old rules for 5-6 players (which included a special building phase). They do not apply to games with 3-4 players.

Actually, the official rule is that once the player reaches 10 victory points during their turn they win and the game ends immediately. So, there is no rule to let the rest of  players who did not take their turn this round (if any) to do it after the winning player declares their victory. The good thing is that no need to worry about ties as they are not possible with such approach. You may try to house rule it, of course.
Sounds good; that makes sense.  Thank you, Maxim!

chunks.thread_subject: Development Cards : University, Palace, Market, Chapel, Library
chunks.embed_text: Do the University, Palace, Market, Chapel, and Library Development Cards belong to an expansion or the base game of Catan, and how are they used?
chunks.content: The University, Palace, Market, Chapel, and Library Development Cards are part of the base game and are used to claim victory when a player reaches 10 VP or more. They are kept hidden during the game and revealed immediately when the goal is reached, including any purchased during the same turn. There was some initial confusion about the rules, but the community clarified that these cards are not played during the game, but rather displayed to prove victory.

raw_thread.body:
I haven't played Catan for a while, but I'm now reviewing it for English teaching purposes. 

I find I have several Development Cards that I don't remember, each wotrth 1 VP :  University, Palace, Market, Chapel, Library .

They have the " Shield " / " circle" (?) reverse side. 

I wonder, do they belong to an expansion ?

No, they are part of the base game and belong with the other development cards. 
In the rules it should specify that you can play one of them immediately after purchase when it gives you your 10th VP. That's unlike the other development cards which you can only play at your next turn at the earliest.

This statement of the rule is poorly written. In CATAN (base version) the player does not PLAY these Victory Point cards during the game! This set of development cards are kept hidden by all players during the game. If a player during his turn attains the goal of 10 VP or more (variations may have a different goal number) the active player declares that he has won and then shows/displays all the Victory Point cards in his hand to prove he has the required number of points to win. The unique rule of these cards differs from other development cards in that the active player shows these cards immediately upon reaching or surpassing the VP goal even the Development Victory Point card that was purchased during this turn (that is do not wait a turn.) These Victory Cards are Revealed (displayed/shown) to claim victory; these cards are NOT played!

chunks.thread_subject: If I have have played 3 knights but then discard one using Vincent's ability (Helpers of Catan expansion), do I lose the "largest army" bonus card?
chunks.embed_text: If a player has the largest army bonus card and uses Vincent's ability from the Helpers of Catan expansion to discard a knight, do they lose the largest army bonus card? The player had 3 knights played and was the only one with knights, but then discarded one knight.
chunks.content: The community is divided on whether the player would lose the largest army bonus card. Some argue that since the player no longer has the requisite 3 knights, they relinquish the Largest Army card. Others point out that the FAQ says 'may' lose the largest army and interpret it as only applying to cases of being tied with another player or losing the minimum number of knights to another player. There is no clear consensus.

raw_thread.body:
This situation came up in my game yesterday (we play base Catan along with the Helpers of Catan scenario cards): 

I had 3 knights already played face-up, and thus I collected the largest army bonus (no one else had any knights played at all).

On my next turn I used Vincent's ability (he lets you discard a face-up knight in order to reduce the necessary resources to build a settlement), which meant I had to  discard a knight. My friend pointed out that this meant I would lose the largest army bonus, because I no longer have 3 knights played.

However the largest army bonus does not include the word "minimum" on it, whereas the longest road bonus does.

So we weren't sure if 3 knights are a minimum to "introduce" the largest army card into play, or if it functions as a minimum to hold the largest army bonus.

Do you think I would have to relinquish the largest army if I used Vincent's ability and dropped down from 3 knights played to 2 knights played?
I would say that since you no longer have the requisite 3 knights, you relinquish the Largest Army card.
You lose the largest army. See the FAQ: https://www.catan.com/faq/catan-helpers

But the FAQ says you "may" lose the largest army. Are you sure that they are referring to the case of going from 3 to 2 knights, or just the case of being tied with another player for most knights and then dropping down to second-most knights?

In my view, the fact that they used "minimum" on the largest roads card but not the largest army doesn't really matter. When the base game was created, Helpers didn't exist; so while there was a way to lose minimum 5 roads (if an opponent built a settlement that divided the roads), there wasn't a way to lose knights. So there was no need to include "minimum" on the largest army card. 
My table would probably treat largest army the same as largest road-- if you lose the number that got the card for you originally, you lose the card. I do see your point and you could certainly make a case for it. But without an official ruling, my table would probably side against your view.

The closest we have to an official ruling are the comments made by Dr Düren in this thread.
On top of that, it's good to realize that Klaus Teuber liked to make like things work alike. Ships work like roads for example. And the largest army works like the longest road. You lose the longest road card if you ever go below the minimum requirement. It's in line with his style to infer that the same would be true for the largest army.
If you drop from 4 knights to 3 knights and no one else has played knights, you won't lose largest army.  That FAQ entry  is not specific to the case of losing one of your 3 knights.

chunks.thread_subject: Starting out
chunks.embed_text: How does the initial placement of roads and settlements work in a 3 or 4 player game of Catan, specifically in terms of the order of placement for each player?
chunks.content: In a 3 or 4 player game, each player places one settlement and adjacent road, then each player places a second one in reverse order. This is explained in the Set-Up Phase of the Almanac, with the specific details found on page 12 for the base game and page 9 for the Star Trek Catan version.

Player 3 go
Player 3 go
Player 2 go
Player 1 go
Start roll?

Yes, you're correct: Each player puts out one settlement and adjacent road, then each player puts out a second one in reverse order. This is explained in the Almanac under "Set-up Phase".

In the current rules at https://www.catan.com/sites/default/files/2021-06/catan_base... , it's under the Set-Up Phase on page 12 of the Almanac.

Round One
The starting player places a settlement ....
The other players then follow clockwise.

Round Two
... the player who went last in the first round begins round two....
Important: After the starting player builds, the other players follow counterclockwise, so the starting player in round one places their second settlement last.

chunks.thread_subject: How many resource cards does a player get when placing a starting settlement this way
chunks.embed_text: How many resource cards does a player get when placing a starting settlement on a space with one or two adjacent resource spaces?
chunks.content: A player gets 1 resource card for each resource space adjacent to their second settlement. If the settlement is adjacent to one resource space, they get 1 card of that resource type. If it's adjacent to two resource spaces, they get 1 card of each resource type. The first settlement placement does not give any resource cards.

raw_thread.body:
Can anyone clarify, if a player were to put their starting settlement on A or B like in this picture, how many resource cards do they get?

A - get 2 resource cards at the start of the game (wheat, lumber)
B - get 1 resource card at the start of the game (brick)

Are these correct?

Yes.  Before the first dice are rolled, the second-placed settlement collects 1 resource card from each resource space to which it is adjacent.  B is adjacent to a single resource space (brick), so it gets 1 brick card.  A is adjacent to 2 resource spaces (wheat, wood), so it collects 1 wheat and 1 wood card.

Assuming those are 2nd settlements.
A = two resources
B = one resource

Ports (neither 1st settlements) give you nothing at the setup.

You're correct, but only if spot A or B is where you placed your second settlement during setup.  Your first placement gets you nothing.  And if you went last, and are effectively placing 2 settlements back to back, you choose which settlement gives you your starting resources.

chunks.thread_subject: If you don't realize that you have the points to win, can you declare victory during someone else's turn?
chunks.embed_text: Can a player declare victory during someone else's turn if they realize they have enough points to win? What if they didn't know they had the points until it was another player's turn?
chunks.content: The community agrees that a player can win the game as soon as they have 10 victory points, but there is some debate about whether they can declare victory during another player's turn. Most posters agree that the player should be given the win, but some argue that they must wait until their next turn to claim victory.

raw_thread.body: Son in law didn't know he had the points to win, during my turn, when I was trying to determine if I could take the longest road from him (a four point swing) he realized his mistake and declared the game over. Is this cool?

The rules say:

Catan You don't have to realize you have those points, although ideally you would. So your son in law won, assuming he had already had the 10 points on his own turn.

In that case, technically he did win.  I would give him the win and try to get him next time 🙂 

PS Welcome to BGG

Give him the win. In a friendly game or family game, other players will know your points and declare you the winner. Some players (having fun) just like to build and expand and don't think of gaining points to win (fun in building and game play.) 

Know that gaining "10" points when it is NOT yet your turn does not end the game nor make you the winner. (Many ways that this can occur - one example: someone else loses the Longest Road bonus and then you get the bonus instead.) After gaining 10 points or more, a player MUST either be the active player (your turn) or wait until your turn to become the winner.

[Cut-throat players (hard-core competition) may argue that you only win if you can declare on your turn - and will state that the "player" failed to declare and thus must wait until the next turn to declare to be the winner.] However, official rules states that the game ENDS when a player has already acquired or achieves the required number of victory points on the player's turn.

rules say:  "If you have - or reach 10 victory points on your turn, the game ends immediately and you win.  You can only win during your turn.  If somehow you find you have 10 victory points during another players turn, you must wait until your next turn to claim victory."

For those who are just learning, I would give him the win, however, I truly believe that if you have 10 points on your turn and you don't claim the win and allow another player to roll the dice, it is not your turn anymore and therefore you must wait until your next turn to claim victory.

chunks.thread_subject: Is giving resources away technically legal?
chunks.embed_text: Is giving resources away in a series of trades technically legal in the game of Catan?
chunks.content: The community is divided on the issue, with some arguing that as long as each individual trade is legal, the series of trades is also legal. Others argue that if the net result of the trades is that one player has given another a resource for free, it should not be allowed, even if it's done through multiple trades. An official ruling states that giving resources away, even in multiple trades, is not allowed due to the potential for collusion.

raw_thread.body:
Would you consider the following trades legal?

Step 1 :Player A trades 2 wood for 1 ore with player B.
Step 2 : Player A trades 1 ore for 1 wood with player B.

Technically, rules as written legal? Yes, it's allowed.

But it feels rather underhanded, whether you "allow" it at your table is down to you and your group. For me personally the net result of those trades is that one player has effectively been given a resource for free and personally I would say that is against the spirit of the rules ("something for nothing" trades are specifically not allowed).

Step 1 :Player A trades 2 wood for 1 ore with player B.
Step 2 : Player A trades 1 ore for 1 wood with player B.

I would say, if you’re doing those trades one after the other, then no, it’s not legal. Because  you’re really just doing one trade which breaks the rules, and trying to justify it by doing it in two stages. But it’s still really one trade. 

Now, if you’re doing step 1 in one turn and wait till the next turn to do step 2, that’s fine, because the dice have been rolled and other trades may have happened in the meantime, so the game state has changed. It’s no longer functionally one trade.
Is it illegal to donate a resource? I have not seen that in the rules.

It's illegal.  Both players have to get something tangible in the trade, and you can't trade a resource for the same resource.

https://www.catan.com/faq
Giving stuff away for free is not allowed.

Step 1 :Player A trades 2 wood for 1 ore with player B.
Step 2 : Player A trades 1 ore for 1 wood with player B.

I would say this is absolutely legal. However, there's nothing stopping player B from changing their mind after the first trade.

It was explicitly stated by the developer that it's not allowed to give other players resources for free. If you want to break the trade down like this in order to circumvent the rules I'd like to remind you that there's also nothing in the rules that says I can't kick you out of my house halfway through the game.

There is one official scenario that allows giving away a resource without being prompted by a card, yielding a reward instead: Frenemies. But only the active player can do so.

Outside of that, it is prohibited.

It is always possible to define a set of rules modifications for your game, ideally set down before play.

ISTM; They have traded two wood for one ore and one wood, Yes, within the rules it is legal.
Question, did Player A gain a consideration for the trade?  Was Player B able to change the game score?

Someone mentioned the "spirit" of the rules above, and I can see examples both ways.

First example

1.  Player A thinks "Oh no, I have eight cards!  Can I sneakily give someone a free card to avoid being caught by the Robber?"
2. Player A trades two wood for one ore with player B.
3. Player A trades one ore for one wood with player B.
4. Player C rolls a 7 and looks grumpily at Players A and B, who both did well out it.

Second example.

1.  Player A is close to building a city and needs two ore.
2. Player A trades two wood for one ore with Player B.
3. Player A tries to get another ore from Player C, who turns out to have none.
4. Player A can't build a city after all and decides to go for a road instead, but now needs a wood.
5. Player B offers to return one of the wood for the ore back.
6. Player A reluctantly agrees.

To me, the first example feels against the spirit of the rules and should't really be allowed.  But the second example feels entirely in the spirit of the game, and ought to be allowed.  Above, Mark L said "The game state has changed. It’s no longer functionally one trade.", and the second example does somewhat fit that since step 3 happened and A gained information from C that no further ore was available.

So, would the second example be allowed?  (If it's both or neither, then I would prefer to allow the first than to ban the second.)

Personally I don't see a legality difference between the following two scenarios:

First Scenario:
1. Player A trades two wood for an ore with player B
2. Player A trades an ore for a wood with player B
Player A has effectively given away a wood to player B. Some people are claiming this is between "against the spirit" and "outright illegal"

Second Scenario:
1. Player A trades two wood for an ore with player B
2. Player A trades an ore for a wood with player C
From Player A's perspective, they have effectively given away a wood, however this is definitely a legal set of trades.

Ultimately its down to whether you consider each individual trade as an independent game event or whether you can take the whole set of trades, and their net outcomes, as one game event.

I honestly don't believe you can seriously enforce the latter. You'd have to track all trades made in a turn, sum everything up, only to then declare "Nope, sorry, after all that you got one sheep for free so take it all back."

If, OP, players A and B continue to exploit the rules like this, then your options are:

- Use the "exploit" yourself

- Tell them that while they're not technically doing anything against the rules, you feel that what they are doing is against the "spirit of the rules" (mention that single something-for-nothing trades are explicitly forbidden) and it is affecting your enjoyment of the game and your play sessions together

...
they're not ... doing anything against the rules,  ...  
single something-for-nothing trades are explicitly forbidden

(Emphasis mine.) The key word is single here. In each individual trade, gifting cards is forbidden. Doing it over multiple trades is legal. The two options are strategically distinct as each player could go back on their non-binding promise to perform the second trade after the first one has been completed, leaving the other player in a worse position if they were counting on the second trade going through.

Player A’s information has changed, but only because they didn’t ask player C if they would trade an ore away before doing the trade with player B. If I was in this situation, I would always check with both players before conducting any trades. If I didn’t, that’s my fault and it doesn’t allow me to tiptoe my way around the rules.

The reason you don’t see the difference is because you’re only looking at it from player A’s perspective, and you’re ignoring the other two players’ perspectives, which also matter. 

In the first example, player B has gained something for nothing, while player A has given something away. In other words, player A has simply given a wood to player B. 

In the second example, player C has gained an ore and lost a wood, while player B has lost an ore and gained two wood. Only player A has not gained anything overall. I think that difference is crucial.

The rule is that you’re not allowed to give something to another player for nothing, not that you’re not allowed to lose overall when you make multiple trades.

Example 1.

Suppose A makes the two-for-one trade with B, and that then there are three turns in which nothing is produced because of die rolls, and nothing is built.  It's now A's turn again, and the game state is exactly as it was before.  Can A now make the one-for-one trade with B?

chunks.thread_subject: What is the tie break?
chunks.embed_text: What is the tiebreaker for players who are not the winner in the game of Settlers of Catan if they have the same score? How are second, third, and fourth places determined in the event of a tie?
chunks.content: There is no official tiebreaker for determining second, third, and fourth places in the game of Settlers of Catan. Some players use house rules, such as the number of cities, settlements, roads, or victory point cards, but these are not officially recognized. The game does not provide a mechanism for ranking players who are not the winner.

raw_thread.body: In the end game, if players (not the winner) get same scores, what is the tie breaker?
You can only win on your turn. You can't have two people taking their turn at the same time, so somebody must win before the other. No tiebreaker is needed.

To win, you have to have the required number of points (10 in the basic game) on your turn.  So there can be no tie.

EDIT:  One minute too slow

He asked what the tie breakers were for the other players, not the winner.  These would separate 2nd, 3rd, and 4th in instances of tied scores.

I actually don't happen to know if there are any such tie breakers in the official rules, but I would assume that some tournament rule sets have established these types of tie breakers.  But again, I don't really know.
D'oh!  You're right, my apologies.  I also do not know of any official rules for this.  I guess we play by the 'if you're not first you're last' motto

In Settlers of Catan, there is no tier. There can be only one winner ever (the player who's turn it is as long as he is among the tied players).

Apart from winning, there is no ranking for 2nd, 3rd ... 

Tournaments (that usually play more than one round) use other means to determine a tournament ranking, but there is none that involves tiers for ranking in a single game. If two players tie for 2nd place with 9 victory points, they are both considered 2nd with 9 victory points.

That's it. Because the game does not offer any means to determine a 2nd and 3rd place in this case

Sebastian Rapp / Kosmos

Number of Cities, then

Victory Point Cards.

chunks.thread_subject: The rules are not clear about starting resources, do you always hand out accourding to the white stars?
chunks.embed_text: How do players determine their starting resources in the game of Catan, and are the white stars on the board used for this purpose?
chunks.content: The community resolved that starting resources are determined by the terrain hexes adjacent to the second settlement placed by each player during the setup stage, with one resource card taken from the supply for each adjacent hex. The white stars are only used for the beginner's setup, and the rulebook's clarity on this point has been noted to vary across editions.

raw_thread.body:
How do we figure out which resources each player starts out with? 3 of us played last night and all read the starting setup rules but could not figure out if you are always supposed to use the white stars for starting resources.

It seems like its just the learning setup and there is another way but we could not find it.

We have played a few times before but the game owner always set things up now we can't figure it out, could some one help us out.

You get one resource from each resource tile adjacent to your starting settlements (so at most you begin with six cards, although depending on placement you may only receive 4 [i.e. if both settlements are on the coast])

You get the resource that are touching the second settlement you lay in setup.

Ex. If the second settlement you play in setup is touching 2 sheep hexes and 1 wood hex, then you get 2 sheep and 1 wood.

If the second settlement you play in setup is touching the desert, a brick hex and a stone hex, then you would get 1 brick and 1 stone.

The rule you're looking for is on page 3: "You receive resources for each terrain hex around your starting settlement marked with a white star..." So, if you're the blue player per the setup illustration, you receive a clay, wool, and ore card. If you do the variable setup (where you place your starting settlements where you choose), you get the resources from the hexes touching the second settlement that you place.

I hope that helps!

Alex Yeager

Mayfair Games

chunks.thread_subject: Robber - discarding half of your cards, do development cards count?
chunks.embed_text: When a player has more than 7 cards and the robber is activated, do development cards count towards the total number of cards that must be discarded?
chunks.content: Development cards do not count as resource cards for the purpose of discarding half of your cards when the robber is activated. The rules state that Resource Cards are discarded, and Development Cards are not considered Resource Cards.

raw_thread.body: When a seven is rolled and the robber is activated, you have to discard half of your cards, if you have more than 7. Do you have to discard half of your cards if you have 5 resource and 3 unplayed development cards, that makes 8 cards in your hand?
No, Development cards do not count as resources for this purpose.  You're fine if you have 5 resource cards in hand and 3 dev cards in hand.
